### Post by cszikszoy on 2008-09-01
When using nvidia-xconfig, you'll need to use sudo so that the program can rewrite your xorg.conf file.

Try this and see if it helps:
```
sudo nvidia-xconfig
```

Note that you should log out and log back in to see if your screen resolution has changed.

If that doesn't fix the problem, can you give me a little more information?  
What resolution is ubuntu currently using?
What resolution should it be using?
If you go to System > Administration > Hardware Drivers, does it list something similar to "NVIDIA accelerated graphics driver..." and is it checked?

---

### Post by doas777 on 2008-09-01
I usually install and use nvidia-settings (with sudo of course).

have you tried setting the refresh to 60? the specs say it will handle 50-76, but 60 is almost always a safe setting.

good luck,
frank

---

### Post by cszikszoy on 2008-09-01
> **doas777 said:**
> I usually install and use nvidia-settings (with sudo of course).

have you tried setting the refresh to 60? the specs say it will handle 50-76, but 60 is almost always a safe setting.

good luck,
frank

I was just going to suggest using nvidia-settings as well.  Install it with:
```
sudo apt-get install nvidia-settings
```

Then it should appear in system > administration > nvidia settings

Use this and it should find all available modes for your graphics card and monitor, then click on "X Server Display Configuration".  Near the bottom right it should have an option to "Save to X Configuration File".

This should rewrite your xorg.conf to use all available modes.

---
